commentary: Spent the morning in Udi LGA with our cooperative partners walking the demonstration plots. Nothing replaces standing in the field with the farmers who grow the food. Grateful for the welcome, as always.
Proud of my postgraduate students who presented their early field results at our departmental seminar this week. Watching them grow into independent researchers is the best part of this job.
The Department of Crop Science will host its annual conference in early October. Submissions on biofortification, agronomy, and smallholder systems are now open to staff and students.
Why vitamin-A biofortified cassava matters: it reaches families through a staple they already grow and eat daily, with no change to their routine. Small science, big reach.
Wonderful to reconnect with collaborators across the region this month. Cross-border research on staple crops is slow work, but the partnerships are what make it durable.
Field season reminder: good data starts with good sampling. We walked our students through randomised plot layout this week and it always pays off at analysis time.
Our next quarterly farmer training workshop in Nsukka LGA is open for registration. Topics include improved planting material, post-harvest handling, and nutrition.
Teaching crop science to undergraduates keeps me honest. If I cannot explain a concept simply at 9am on a Monday, I do not understand it well enough yet.
Long lab day, but the carotenoid assays are finally running smoothly again. Shout-out to the technicians who keep our instruments alive through every power cut.
Congratulations to our Crop Science staff on a strong year of grant-funded research. Proud of the work coming out of this department.
Reading about how other regions scale biofortification adoption. The technology is rarely the bottleneck; trust, seed access, and extension support usually are.
Mid-grant highlight: vitamin-A cassava trials in South-East Nigeria are entering their second year. Encouraging early engagement from cooperative farmers.
Bi-weekly call with our Nairobi partners on the yam programme. Different crop, same lesson: improvement work only sticks when the people growing the crop are part of the design.
Great to see strong tropical-crop science coming out of West and East African institutions. The most interesting plant research right now is happening close to the field.
Sharing a few practical notes from our cassava processing trials. Happy to compare methods with colleagues working on retention of pro-vitamin A.
A quiet research day with the door closed and a strong cup of coffee. Manuscripts do not write themselves, sadly. Back to the field tomorrow.
Applications are open for postgraduate research positions in the Faculty of Agriculture for the next academic session. Several positions in crop and soil science.
Another full house at our farmer training session in Nsukka LGA this week. The questions from the cooperatives keep getting sharper every quarter.
To my students heading into exams: you are not behind, you are exactly where careful work takes time to be. Steady and thorough beats fast and frantic.
Looking forward to hosting more cross-institutional exchanges this year. Regional collaboration is how African agricultural research compounds.
